text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating room table. The surgeon initiates the procedure by establishing pneumoperitoneum using a Veress needle inserted through the umbilicus.
An 8mm trocar is placed in the peri-umbilical area, followed by inspection of the abdominal cavity, which shows no evidence of injury from the entry point.

Dense adhesions of the omentum and bowel are visible attached to the anterior abdominal wall. The surgical team places two additional robotic trocars in the right abdomen and left upper abdomen, along with an assistant port in the left lower quadrant.
The robotic surgical system is brought in and docked from the left side of the patient, preparing for the next phase of the procedure.

The surgeon dissects and removes dense inflammatory adhesions in the upper abdomen to improve visualization. Due to the severity of the adhesions, the gallbladder is deemed inaccessible, leading to the decision to abandon the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon clears part of the body of the stomach, providing adequate space for the procedure, while much of the stomach remains obscured.
The surgeon creates a gastrostomy to open the stomach, revealing the lumen. The previously placed endoscopic stent is visible, with some debris observed clogging the stent.
The surgeon clears dense adhesions and removes the endoscopic stents from the stomach. The systolic gastrostomy tract is expanded to further open the stomach lumen.

The surgeon encounters brisk bleeding while gradually penetrating the stomach wall, initially unable to control it with a bipolar device. Pressure is applied to establish temporary control, and the bleeding is ultimately managed with two figure-of-eight sutures.
The surgeon continues to expand the cystogastrostomy, reinforcing the newly created edges with two silk sutures. A running PDA suture is then used to secure the edges of the cystogastrostomy.
The surgeon washes out the cavity and removes necrotic material from the cyst, performing a necrosectomy.
The surgeon closes the anterior gastrotomy using running sutures in a canal fashion, with visible estimated blood loss of approximately 300 mL.
